channel.title: NYT > Books > Book Review
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: A Traveling Bookstore Keeps on Rolling
item.description: Rita Collins had a dream for her retirement: bringing books and people together all over the country. Behind the wheel of a van she’s making it happen.
item.dc:creator: Ruth Fremson

item.title: Book Review: ‘Look What You Made Me Do,’ by John Lanchester
item.description: In John Lanchester’s “Look What You Made Me Do,” a widow is unnerved when a hit TV series airs details from her marriage a little too closely.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Jacobs
item.category: Look What You Made Me Do (Book)

item.title: Poetry Review: ‘Wellwater,’ by Karen Solie
item.description: The Canadian poet Karen Solie balances environmental concerns with hope and deadpan wit.
item.dc:creator: Daisy Fried

item.title: The Best Books to Read on American Empire
item.description: As these books show, the United States has long struggled to reconcile its imperial ambition with its founding ideals, prompting detractors at home and abroad.
item.dc:creator: Alexis Coe
item.category: How to Hide an Empire: A History of the Greater United States (Book)
A Wicked War: Polk, Clay, Lincoln, and the 1846 U.S. Invasion of Mexico (Book)
Heroes to Hostages: America and Iran, 18001988 (Book)
The Brothers: John Foster Dulles, Allen Dulles, and Their Secret World War: John Foster Dulles, Allen Dulles, and Their Secret World War (Book)
Polar War: Submarines, Spies, and the Struggle for Power in a Melting Arctic (Book)

item.title: Book Review: ‘Men Like Ours,’ by Bindu Bansinath
item.description: “Men Like Ours,” a novel by Bindu Bansinath, follows an immigrant family through a community crisis.
item.dc:creator: Dwight Garner

item.title: Amitav Ghosh Brings the Main Character of ‘Ghost Eye’ to Life, With the Help of a Sketch Artist
item.description: With the help of a forensic artist, Amitav Ghosh puts a face to the name of Varsha Gupta, the central figure of his new novel, “Ghost Eye.”
item.dc:creator: Jenny Comita

item.title: Book Review: ‘When the Forest Breathes,’ by Suzanne Simard
item.description: Suzanne Simard’s new book urges Western science to take a lesson from the more holistic Indigenous approach to forest preservation.
item.dc:creator: Deborah Blum
item.category: When the Forest Breathes: Renewal and Resilience in the Natural World (Book)

item.title: Book Review: ‘Selling Opportunity,’ by Mary Lisa Gavenas
item.description: In “Selling Opportunity,” Mary Lisa Gavenas tells the not-always-rosy story of Mary Kay, the brand — and its founder.
item.dc:creator: Marisa Meltzer
item.category: Selling Opportunity: The Story of Mary Kay (Book)
item.media:description: Although in many ways a sales pioneer, Mary Kay Ash was a traditionalist who preferred that her fleet of saleswomen only work for fun rather than threaten their husbands’ egos.

item.title: Edith Eva Eger, Psychologist Who Barely Survived Auschwitz, Dies at 98
item.description: Her time in concentration camps brought her an understanding of humanity that helped her treat her patients.
item.dc:creator: Richard Sandomir

item.title: J.H. Prynne, Erudite and Elusive British Poet, Dies at 89
item.description: Even admirers admitted his densely intellectual work could be “punishing.” Still, some considered him one of England’s most important poets.
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams
item.media:description: J.H. Prynne in a rare photograph in 2004. A notoriously dense and intellectual poet, he disclosed virtually nothing of his personal life and refused to pose for the camera, even for his book jackets.

item.title: Xia De-hong, 94, Dies; Persecuted in China, She Starred in Daughter’s Memoir
item.description: “Wild Swans,” a best-selling 1991 memoir, told the story of a stoic mother holding her family together amid torture and imprisonment under Mao’s regime.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nossiter
item.category: Wild Swans: Three Daughters of China (Book)

item.title: How ‘The Sheep Detectives’ Brought its Ovine Sleuths to Life
item.description: The filmmakers behind this adaptation of a best-selling novel were adamant that their ovine sleuths not seem like humans in, well, sheep’s clothing.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Lyall

item.title: Why Is Everyone Obsessed With Bogs?
item.description: From fashion to art, an explainer on our love of wetlands.
item.dc:creator: Zoey Poll

item.title: How Children’s Picture Books Comfort Harried Parents
item.description: As a child, Lisa Owens aligned herself with the little ones depicted in these books; now it was the harried adults who captivated her.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Owens
item.category: Alexander and the Terrible, Horrible, No Good, Very Bad Day (Book)

item.title: Book Review: ‘One Leg on Earth,’ by ‘Pemi Aguda
item.description: In “One Leg on Earth,” a young college grad’s idealistic move to Lagos turns into a nightmare.
item.dc:creator: Ainslie Hogarth

item.title: Book Review: ‘Screen People,’ by Megan Garber
item.description: In “Screen People,” Megan Garber looks at how we all became famous for 15 minutes.
item.dc:creator: Madison Malone Kircher
item.category: Screen People: How We Entertained Ourselves Into a State of Emergency (Book)
item.media:credit: Dmitry Kostyukov for The New York Times
item.media:description: Thanks to the personal Hollywood studio neatly contained in your cellphone, writes Mega Garber, Garber’s core argument is that the language and ethos of traditional entertainment has seeped into every aspect of our lives.

item.title: Historical Fiction Books That Illustrate the Bonds Between Mother and Child
item.description: The best-selling author Stephanie Dray recommends books that explore the bonds between mothers and their children across centuries.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Dray
item.media:credit: Image by The New York Times; Author photo by Kelsey Edwards

item.title: Karen Tei Yamashita Writes About Japanese American Internment in Her New Novel
item.description: In her sprawling new novel, Karen Tei Yamashita sprinkles fanciful details (a trombone narrator!) into the bracing story of World War II internment.
item.dc:creator: Robert Ito
item.media:credit: Arsenii Vaselenko for The New York Times
item.media:description: “Questions 27 & 28” is Karen Tei Yamashita’s first novel in 16 years.

item.title: Philip Caputo, Who Wrote Blistering Vietnam War Memoir, Dies at 84
item.description: “A Rumor of War,” about his service as a Marine Corps infantry officer and published in 1977, relentlessly detailed “the things men do in war and the things war does to them.”
item.dc:creator: Joseph Berger
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Frank Lennon/Toronto Star, via Getty Images
item.media:description: Philip Caputo in 1987. “To call it the best book about Vietnam is to trivialize it,” the novelist and screenwriter John Gregory Dunne wrote in his review of “A Rumor of War.” “Heartbreaking, terrifying and enraging, it belongs to the literature of men at arms.”

item.title: Books Our Editors Love This Week
item.description: Reading recommendations from critics and editors at The New York Times.
item.category: The Successor: Boris Nemtsov, Vladimir Putin and the Decline of Modern Russia (Book)
The Family Man: Blood and Betrayal in the House of Murdaugh (Book)

item.title: Book Review: ‘Revenge for the Sixties,’ by Peter S. Canellos; ‘Alito,’ by Mollie Hemingway
item.description: Two new biographies of the Supreme Court justice show how his career was propelled by a legal movement that coalesced to take down Roe v. Wade.
item.dc:creator: Mary Jo McConahay
item.category: Revenge for the Sixties: Sam Alito and the Triumph of the Conservative Legal Movement (Book)
Alito: The Justice Who Reshaped the Supreme Court and Restored the Constitution (Book)
item.media:description: Samuel Alito at the swearing-in ceremony for the defense secretary Mark Esper (not pictured) in 2019.

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Last Contract of Isako,’ by Fonda Lee
item.description: Fonda Lee’s “cyberpunk samurai in space” novel follows a sword-wielding warrior trying to finish one last job.
item.dc:creator: Amal El-Mohtar

item.title: Book Review: ‘From Life Itself,’ by Suzy Hansen
item.description: In a new book, the journalist Suzy Hansen plumbs an Istanbul neighborhood to better understand Turkey’s hard-right turn.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Szalai
item.category: From Life Itself: Turkey, Istanbul, and a Neighborhood in the Age of Erdogan (Book)
item.media:description: A poster of Turkey’s president, Recep Tayyip Erdogan, above a street in Rize, Turkey, in 2016.

item.title: In ‘Rocky Horror,’ Luke Evans Finds His Ballad of Sexual Liberation
item.description: What does it take to play Frank-N-Furter in “The Rocky Horror Show” on Broadway? Luke Evans transforms in five-inch heels and an endless supply of glitter.
item.dc:creator: Zachary Small and Justin J Wee
item.media:credit: Justin J Wee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The actor’s transformation includes makeup design by Sterling Tull and David I. Reynoso’s costumes.

item.title: Sizzling Summer Thrillers
item.description: Our columnist on the month’s standout books.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Lyall

item.title: In Her New Memoir, Siri Hustvedt Captures Life With, And Without, Paul Auster
item.description: Until now, in a new memoir that has Siri Hustvedt writing about the highs, lows and late-life tragedies of their glamorous literary marriage.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter
item.media:credit: Ahmed Gaber for The New York Times
item.media:description: Siri Hustvedt began writing her new memoir soon after the death of Paul Auster, her husband and a fellow novelist.

item.title: Epic Fantasy and Sci-Fi Books for Adults
item.description: The best-selling author Fonda Lee recommends fantasy and science fiction novels with older, wiser, absolutely epic heroes.
item.dc:creator: Fonda Lee

item.title: Five Publishers and Scott Turow Sue Meta and Mark Zuckerberg
item.description: The class-action lawsuit accuses the tech giant and its founder and chief executive of infringing on authors’ copyrights.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra Alter
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Family Man,’ by James Lasdun
item.description: “The Family Man,” by the novelist and poet James Lasdun, brings a literary voice and elaborate detail to a case that gripped the nation.
item.dc:creator: Orlando Whitfield
item.category: The Family Man: Blood and Betrayal in the House of Murdaugh (Book)
item.media:description: Alex Murdaugh on March 2, 2023, after being convicted of murdering his wife and son.

item.title: Book Review: ‘The Hill,’ by Harriet Clark
item.description: Partly inspired by her life, Harriet Clark’s “The Hill” portrays a young girl navigating between her beloved mother’s jail cell and the world outside.
item.dc:creator: Hermione Hoby

item.title: Book Review: ‘List of All Possible Desires,’ by Dylan Landis
item.description: In a new novel told in interlinked stories, Dylan Landis revisits a dauntless family she has written about since 2009.
item.dc:creator: Joan Silber
item.category: List of All Possible Desires: A Novel in Stories (Book)

item.title: Book Review: ‘Riverwork,’ by Lisa Robertson
item.description: “Riverwork,” by Lisa Robertson, considers the lost history of the Bièvre and the lives of working women once linked to it.
item.dc:creator: Kerry Howley

item.title: Book Review: ‘Prestige Drama,’ by Seamas O’Reilly
item.description: Séamas O’Reilly’s new novel is a boisterous sendup of “prestige” media and its distortion of Northern Ireland’s complex past.
item.dc:creator: Colin Barrett
